artifacts.content:
Hi Emily,

I hope it’s okay to email rather than wait until the next appointment. Things have been a bit up and down again and I wanted to give you a clearer picture before we catch up.

Since that emergency appointment on 14 June 2024 at Hawkesbury District Health Service, when I told you about driving over North Richmond Bridge thinking about turning the ute into the river with me in it and we wrote out the safety plan Sarah keeps in the top drawer of our bedside table at Unit 5, 18 Lawson Crescent, the thoughts had settled for a while. Lately they’ve crept back in more, especially after long days at Hawkesbury Logistics & Storage.

The worst was 12 October 2025, when I drove out to the Yarramundi Reserve lookout just after midnight and sat there for about 2 hours seriously thinking about driving off the embankment. I ended up texting Anna around 1:30am saying I didn’t see the point of anything anymore and she called Lifeline with me on speaker from her place in Penrith. Since then it’s mostly been passive stuff – “not wanting to wake up” type thoughts – but it rattled me how close I felt that night.

Sleep is still rough. I’m waking around 3–4am most mornings with the same racing thoughts about work, money, and losing the unit in Richmond, and then feel like a zombie at the warehouse. The sertraline 100 mg has helped with irritability, and the extra 25 mg of quetiapine you added back in early April 2024 did settle the 3am replaying of that 2019 ANZAC Day argument with Dad a bit, but the early waking is creeping back.

When we meet next, could we review the safety plan and maybe talk about other options for the sleep and panic? I’m still taking the irbesartan 150 mg in the mornings and trying not to overdo the Panadol after that scare in March 2024.

Thanks,
Michael

metadata.subject: Recent night at Yarramundi and sleep issues

artifacts.content:
Hey Sar,

Just hiding in the lunchroom for five minutes before I have to go back out on the floor, thought I’d catch you up properly instead of just sending half-baked texts.

Work’s been a pressure cooker since that formal written warning from Mark Stevens on 3 September 2025 here at the South Windsor warehouse. He had me in his office about the $18,000 pallet miscount and the 7 sick days I’d taken in the previous three months. Ever since that meeting, my chest tightens whenever an email from head office pops up. I’ve been getting those panic-y episodes at least once a week, usually when I’m doing stock checks.

I keep thinking about that earlier one on the loading dock back on 3 May 2024 – when I froze, dropped the pallet and Steve sent me home on the night shift, then gave me the informal warning about my supervisor role and overtime being on the line if it happened again. Feels like I’m always one mistake away from losing it all.

The long shifts aren’t helping my back either. I’m trying to be careful since that weekend in March 2024 when I pushed through a double shift, took about 6 grams of Panadol in 24 hours and ended up at Hawkesbury District emergency on 17 March for monitoring. Emily’s been on at me about not using pain meds like that again.

On top of that I’m still waking at 3–4am worrying about the Westpac mortgage arrears notice we got on 21 July 2025 for Lawson Crescent and having to borrow the $2,000 from Dad. I keep picturing Liv having to leave Richmond High and Jake leaving Richmond Public if we lose the place.

Anyway, better get back out there. Can you grab milk on the way home? We’re nearly out.

Love,
M

Hi Dad,

Sorry it’s taken me a bit to write this properly instead of just mumbling about it over the phone.

Thanks again for helping us out with the mortgage when Westpac sent that 30‑day arrears notice on 21 July 2025 for the unit at 18 Lawson Crescent. I still feel awful that I had to borrow the $2,000 from you to cover the missed payments and late fees. Since that letter came, I’ve been waking up around 3–4am most mornings with my chest tight and my stomach in knots, picturing us losing the place and Olivia having to leave Richmond High and Jacob leaving Richmond Public.

It all sits on top of the other stuff that’s been building the last couple of years – the long-standing low mood, anxiety, and the panic-like episodes. You know about the emergency appointment with Dr Hart on 14 June 2024 at Hawkesbury District when I told her I’d been driving over North Richmond Bridge thinking about turning the ute into the river, and she helped me put a written safety plan together. Since then there’ve been a few bad patches, like the blow-up on 27 August 2024 when I yelled at Liv over a B in maths and at Jake about his boots, and Sarah took them to stay at her mum’s in North Richmond for the night.

I’m working with Emily on getting better – on sertraline 100 mg, irbesartan 150 mg for the blood pressure, and a bit of quetiapine at night for sleep. I’ve also started seeing a psychologist again to try to get on top of the guilt and the “I’m useless” thoughts.

I really appreciate you and Mum having our backs. I’m hoping once things steady up I can start paying you back in small chunks.

Love,
Michael

metadata.subject: Thank you (and a bit of an update)

artifacts.content:
Personal journal – 2 September 2025

Need to get some of this out of my head.

The last year or two feels like it’s been one long slide. Low mood most days, no energy, everything feels like “moving through wet cement.” I’ve basically stopped soccer and fishing, barely see mates. Since that panic episode driving the kids home from Richmond Public in late August 2023, when I had that flash of steering the car into a tree on Kurrajong Road, I’ve been more jumpy about anything to do with driving and suicide talk. Emily warned me that detailed discussions of methods or graphic accident stories can set off those intrusive images, so I need to be careful getting home after any heavy sessions.

There have been other scary moments too: November 2022, scratching my left forearm with the box cutter in the garage at Lawson Crescent after the argument with Sarah about money and my hours; the early June 2023 faint in the staff lunchroom at Hawkesbury Logistics after standing about three hours, then taking my irbesartan late without food; March 2024 when I pushed through a double shift, took around 6 g of paracetamol in 24 hours and ended up at Hawkesbury District ED on 17 March for monitoring.

On top of that, the parent–teacher with Olivia’s Year 7 coordinator, Mrs Karen Lewis, at Richmond High on 28 August 2025 is stuck in my head. Hearing that Liv is worried about “Dad being sad all the time” feeds the guilt loops that keep me up until 2am.

Goals: keep using the safety plan, be honest with Emily about the self‑harm urges, ask any group program to let me step out if family-argument role‑plays or graphic suicide stuff start to spin me out, and remember to sit or lie down if I feel lightheaded.

Hey Anna,

Kids are finally in bed and Sarah’s half-asleep on the couch, so I’ve got a quiet moment.

I’ve been thinking a lot about that night on 12 October 2025 when I drove out to the Yarramundi Reserve lookout just after midnight and sat there for about two hours, seriously thinking about driving off the embankment. I still can’t believe I actually sent you that text at around 1:30am saying I didn’t see the point of anything anymore. I’m so grateful you picked up from Penrith and stayed on the phone while we called Lifeline together.

It’s like that was the peak of a build-up that started way back. The November 2022 argument with Sarah about money and my hours, ending with me scratching my forearm with the box cutter in the garage at Lawson Crescent. The panic episode driving the kids home from Richmond Public in late August 2023 on Kurrajong Road, where I had that split-second thought about steering into a tree. The loading dock panic on 3 May 2024 when I froze, dropped the pallet and Steve sent me home, warning my supervisor role and overtime could be at risk. Then the formal written warning from Mark Stevens on 3 September 2025 at the South Windsor warehouse over the $18k pallet error and 7 sick days.

Chuck in the Westpac 30‑day arrears notice on 21 July 2025 for Lawson Crescent, borrowing the $2,000 from Dad, and that parent–teacher with Mrs Lewis at Richmond High on 28 August about Liv being worried about “Dad being sad all the time,” and it’s no wonder my head feels like it’s going to explode some days.

I’m trying to hang onto the small stuff – Friday takeaway, kicking the footy with Jake when my back allows, helping Liv with homework. Your texts reminding me therapy helped you do make a difference.

Love,
Mick

Note to self – therapy goals and reminders (updated 5 November 2025)

1. Suicidal thoughts
- Aim: get back to where things were just after the emergency appointment with Emily on 14 June 2024 at Hawkesbury District, when the thoughts about driving off North Richmond Bridge settled down after we wrote the safety plan Sarah keeps in the bedside drawer.
- Use plan if thoughts about Yarramundi lookout (12 Oct 2025) or Kurrajong Road tree crash (late Aug 2023) pop up.

2. Panic/anxiety at work
- Remember the 3 May 2024 loading dock incident and 3 September 2025 formal warning with Mark at the South Windsor warehouse (pallet miscount, $18k, 7 sick days). Goal: reduce panic attacks from weekly to less than once a month using breathing, grounding, and better pacing.

3. Family and guilt
- Key memories: 27 August 2024 blow-up with Liv’s B in maths at Richmond High and Jake’s boots; Sarah taking them to her mum’s in North Richmond. 28 August 2025 parent–teacher with Mrs Karen Lewis where Liv said she’s worried about “Dad being sad all the time.”
- Goal: learn to de‑escalate, apologise quickly, and schedule positive time (Friday movie night, Sunday park trips) so the kids don’t feel like they have to walk on eggshells.

4. Sleep and meds
- Still on sertraline 100 mg, irbesartan 150 mg, quetiapine 25 mg at night. Watch that I don’t repeat the 6 g Panadol weekend before Hawkesbury District ED on 17 March 2024.
- Target: 6–7 hours sleep most nights, fewer 3–4am wake-ups about mortgage (Westpac arrears 21 July 2025, $2,000 from Dad).

Event: GP review – mood, sleep, back pain, meds

Date/Time: Monday 19 January 2026, 4:10–4:40 p.m.
Location: Riverside Family Medical Clinic, Suite 3, 214 Bridge Street, Richmond NSW 2753 (Dr Emily Hart – provider no. 4583921B, phone (02) 4578-2299, fax (02) 4578-2301, email emily.hart@riversidemedical.com.au)

Notes / agenda:

- Update Emily on recent mood: most days still low, but some better mornings. Mention passive suicidal thoughts a few times a week, especially after long shifts at Hawkesbury Logistics & Storage. Remind her about the 12 October 2025 Yarramundi Reserve lookout night and that I used the safety plan we wrote after the 14 June 2024 North Richmond Bridge appointment.

- Sleep: still waking around 3–4 a.m. worrying about work, Westpac mortgage arrears (21 July 2025 letter for Unit 5, 18 Lawson Crescent, borrowed $2,000 from Dad), and kids’ schools (Olivia at Richmond High, Jacob at Richmond Public). Ask if timing of sertraline 100 mg or quetiapine 25 mg needs tweaking.

- Anxiety/panic: talk about weekly panic-like episodes since Mark’s formal warning on 3 September 2025 at the South Windsor warehouse (pallet miscount $18k, 7 sick days) and history of loading dock incident 3 May 2024. Ask for written strategies I can keep in my phone because I sometimes blank on verbal instructions, especially in busy waiting rooms like Richmond VHA around 4–5 p.m.

- Physical health: review irbesartan 150 mg, back pain management, and make sure liver function still okay after the 6 g Panadol in 24 hrs / Hawkesbury District ED visit on 17 March 2024. Mention fainting episode in staff lunchroom (early June 2023) after 3 hrs standing and late irbesartan without food.

Bring: Medicare card (2953 76482 1, exp 11/2027), list of questions, safety plan copy.
